HOSTBUDDY - MULTI-TENANT CLOUD FOR AGENTIC COMMERCE

CommercePlex

Real-Time Multi-Tenant RL Infrastructure for Agentic Commerce

Sub-300ms
Decision latency target
60 Days
Execution timeline
Multi-tenant
Merchant isolation + scale

Project Objective

Develop a production-grade, multi-tenant AI infrastructure for agentic commerce capable of:

  • Serving thousands of merchants concurrently using tenant-aware adapter orchestration with strict merchant isolation.
  • Achieving sub-300ms decision latency for live voice ordering, checkout interventions, and cart updates.
  • Training and evaluating RL-driven policies for complex upsells, combo optimization, and conversion-aware guidance.
  • Benchmarking conversational voice models for real-time commerce, including NVIDIA voice systems versus external baselines such as PlayHT.
  • Hardening transactional AI pipelines against prompt injection, unsafe tool use, price overrides, and cross-tenant data leakage.

Model / System Architecture Overview

ComponentDetails
Base Inference LayerShared LLM and voice serving stack running on NVIDIA GPU infrastructure for high-concurrency commerce workloads.
Multi-Tenant Adapter RoutingLoRAx-style adapter exchange to apply merchant-specific menus, policies, promotions, and behavioral preferences without one model per merchant.
Low-Latency Execution PathDeterministic transaction loop for order capture, modifier validation, cart updates, and checkout-critical actions, separated from slower reasoning flows.
RL Policy EngineGRPO-based optimization for upsells, combo suggestions, cross-sells, and cart recovery based on merchant goals and live context.
Voice Benchmark HarnessTest harness for commerce voice tasks comparing NVIDIA conversational voice systems with external real-time baselines such as PlayHT.
Context / Retrieval LayerRuntime injection of merchant menus, modifiers, prices, inventory state, time-of-day offers, loyalty rules, and seasonal promotions.
Pre-Inference Sanitization LayerOWASP-aligned enforcement layer for blocking prompt injection, tool misuse, invalid discounts, price manipulation, and data exfiltration.
Observability / EvaluationInstrumentation for p50/p95 latency, task completion, GPU utilization, merchant density, voice naturalness, and conversion lift.

Data Sources

Commerce Data

  • Merchant menus and modifier trees
  • Large catalog / inventory structures
  • Pricing and discount rules
  • Promotions, loyalty entitlements, and time-sensitive offers
  • Historical order flows and cart sequences
  • Voice and text interaction traces
  • Merchant policy constraints

Synthetic Data

Synthetic data will play a central role in training and evaluation, analogous to how simulation is used in other NVIDIA workflows, except our domain is complex commerce interactions rather than video scenes.

  • Complex combo orders from large restaurant menus such as Cheesecake Factory-style catalogs
  • Large-inventory shopping flows similar to Home Depot-scale product trees
  • Procedurally generated order conversations with long modifier chains, conflicting preferences, out-of-stock substitutions, upsell opportunities, interrupted orders, and noisy caller behavior
  • Auto-labeled outcomes for item selection correctness, modifier completeness, cart accuracy, upsell relevance, conversion outcome, and policy violations

This synthetic workload is especially important because real-world Main Street data is fragmented, inconsistent, and merchant-specific. We want to use DGX Cloud resources to generate and replay high-volume, labeled commerce interactions for both RL training and inference benchmarking.

NVIDIA Hardware / Software Stack Integration

1. NVIDIA DGX Cloud / Innovation Lab Infrastructure

Use the Innovation Lab’s developer-friendly self-serve NVIDIA GPU environment for high-concurrency inference benchmarking and RL training. Validate merchant-density, latency, and serving economics under production-like load. Run large synthetic commerce datasets and replay workloads at scale.

2. NVIDIA AI Enterprise

Use NVIDIA AI Enterprise as the core software platform for building, deploying, and scaling the application stack. Leverage its supported application and infrastructure layers for repeatable deployment, orchestration, and observability.

3. NVIDIA NIM

Evaluate NIM inference microservices as a path for secure, reliable deployment of optimized model endpoints for commerce tasks. Compare serving architectures for language and voice components where relevant.

4. NVIDIA NeMo

Use NeMo for RL-related workflows, fine-tuning, evaluation, policy optimization, and observability around agent behavior. Explore agent lifecycle tooling for training and improving domain-specific commerce agents.

5. TensorRT / TensorRT-LLM

Optimize latency-critical inference paths for voice turn response, structured order capture, and checkout interventions. Benchmark precision / throughput tradeoffs for production serving.

Voice Model Benchmarking Track

A dedicated workstream in this project is benchmarking commerce-grade voice systems in real ordering environments.

Time-to-first-audio End-to-end turn latency Interruption / barge-in handling Order capture accuracy Modifier completeness Persona consistency Naturalness Task completion rate Conversion impact Cost per successful session

We plan to compare NVIDIA conversational voice systems, including PersonaPlex-style full-duplex approaches where applicable, with external real-time baseline systems such as PlayHT. The goal is not just to optimize HostBuddy’s voice stack, but also to provide NVIDIA voice teams with applied feedback from fast, interruption-heavy, structured commerce conversations.

Execution Timeline – 60 Days

PhaseDurationTasks
Phase 1: SetupWeek 1Set up DGX Cloud environment, inference stack, synthetic commerce data pipeline, observability framework, and baseline commerce benchmark harness.
Phase 2: Synthetic Data GenerationWeeks 2–3Generate synthetic order flows for large menus and large inventories; create labeled scenarios for combo orders, substitutions, interruptions, and upsell opportunities.
Phase 3: Multi-Tenant + RL TrainingWeeks 3–5Train and evaluate merchant-aware policy layers using synthetic and historical commerce data; benchmark adapter-routing behavior and tenant isolation.
Phase 4: Voice BenchmarkingWeeks 4–5Compare NVIDIA voice stack versus PlayHT-style streaming baseline across latency, interruption handling, order accuracy, and naturalness in live commerce scenarios.
Phase 5: OptimizationWeeks 5–6Optimize inference using TensorRT / TensorRT-LLM / NIM-compatible deployment paths; reduce latency for voice and transaction loops; measure throughput and cost efficiency.
Phase 6: EvaluationWeek 7Validate on realistic merchant workloads; measure p95 latency, task completion, merchant density, upsell lift, and security robustness.
Phase 7: FinalizationWeek 8Package benchmark results, deployment reference architecture, evaluation report, and recommendations for scaling agentic commerce on NVIDIA infrastructure.

Expected Deliverables

  • Multi-tenant commerce inference prototype
  • Synthetic commerce dataset for large-menu and large-inventory order workflows
  • RL policy optimization report for upsell / conversion / combo handling
  • Voice benchmarking report comparing NVIDIA voice systems with external baseline systems
  • Latency / throughput / merchant-density benchmark report
  • Security evaluation report for transactional safety
  • Deployment reference architecture for agentic commerce on NVIDIA infrastructure
  • Technical blog or case study summarizing findings

Why This Project Matters

Main Street commerce still does not broadly use agentic AI. The blockers are not just model quality; they are cost, latency, security, and domain complexity. HostBuddy is building the missing infrastructure layer that can make agentic commerce deployable through the commerce platforms and integrations that already reach millions of merchants.

This project is a strong fit for NVIDIA because it combines multi-tenant inference economics, low-latency transaction execution, reinforcement-learning-based revenue optimization, and a difficult real-world voice benchmark.

It is not a generic chatbot use case. It is a production infrastructure problem for one of the largest under-automated markets in AI.
